entries.prompt: Do you often overreact in stressful situations? Reflect on a situation in which your thoughts and feelings have led to an overreaction. What do you think you could have done differently?
entries.response:
I could have snapped my son out of talking over me without yelling.
In team meeting I could have been more positive about x-mas party and kept bias thoughts from being expressed.
I have to stop estimating that events I don't want to attend will be a waste of time.
Disappointment in my kids current behaviour assumes that it will become exponentially worse and my reaction now is measured against that estimate.
Playing parents off one another is a teenage thing, not a larger manipulation or disrespect.

entries.prompt: Does your stress, overthinking, or emotional reactions lead to poor decision-making on your part?
Think about a time when detachment could have led you to make better decisions. What could you have done in that moment to better focus on the control of your thoughts and emotions?
entries.response:
Detaching would prevent the blowing up of the problem into a worse case reaction that hasn't happened yet and is not warranted.
Breathing exercises.
Letting them finish completely their argument and take a calm posture.
Curiosity demands patience.
Curiosity demands being open to ideas.
Curiosity is learning which removes the time wasting aspect.
Could have taken a time-out to consider things as there was no deadline.

entries.prompt: Think of a time when you lack of acceptance to a situation led to frustration and behaviour on your part that was counter-productive. What could you have done differently if you had practiced acceptance? How might that have changed the outcome.
entries.response:
Instructed a different way to do something.
Push-back or ignoring the instruction lead to disrespect.
Verbalising dissatisfaction leads to poor moral in team.
Give it a go.
Curious about a result.
May have proven to be right or wrong.
May have inspired others with can-do attitude.

entries.prompt: Part of acceptance means giving up on the idea of perfection. What are some ways that you irrationally hold on to the idea of perfection. How could you reframe your thoughts to accept the idea that nothing is perfect?
entries.response:
Give up the idea of having zero contradictions, and the pride I take in it.
Give up 'my way is the right way'.
Give up most efficient being the best.
Let go of my "Family advice is always right".
Let kids understand that there are plenty of times yet to do better or be perfect.
Treat perfection as being a moment in time and seek out other moments that may have passed me by.

entries.prompt: Think about an incident from your past that you are holding on to. What makes it hard to let go? What would happen if you looked at it rationally? Use the space below to work out the situation dispassionately as if it happened to someone else. Consider whether or not you can accept this incident as something that happened in the past and move on from it.
entries.response:
Betrayed by friend who was drug addict.
Hard to let go because it impacted another friend who was innocent, ruined my reputation with that friend and whole friend group. Felt heartbroken and worthless when sold out for a few thousand dollars.
Rationally the person was afflicted with cravings that sold me out for the next hit. It was not personal from their side.
My innocent friend & wider group moved on against me, my reputation is just my perception of what people think. I can control that.
This has passed and I can move on.

entries.prompt: List the things about yourself that you'd like to change? List on the right whether these are inside or outside your control.
entries.response:
Challenges (left column) | Learnings & Opportunities (right column)
Health → Inside, prioritise what I am consuming
Procrastination → Inside, plan and list
Culling of property → Inside, commit time  –  get help
Inspiration for kids → Inside and outside,  keep improving
Crooked teeth → Inside, commit time/money
Dry Skin → Inside and outside, list daily regime
Back Pain → Inside and outside, lose weight
Temper → Inside, journal & practice
Cynicism → Inside, keep to self, park harsh
Negativity → Inside, separate thoughts
Oppositional → Inside, practice their side first
Drive-shaming → Inside, practice holding my opinion
Observational → Inside, direct toward writing
Hesitant → Inside, weekly challenge
Anti-Social → Inside, meet up with someone
Dark Thoughts → Inside and outside, ID them & distract
Nature Walks → Inside, schedule to do them
Appreciation → Inside, reflect/take pause

entries.prompt: What are some of the ways you can balance your efforts to achieve virtue through acceptance of yourself?
entries.response:
Accept I haven't always treated others with fairness & respect despite 'doing the right thing' being what I stand for.
Accept that despite every effort some people/outcomes/perceptions cannot be reached.
Accept that I haven't as yet taken action that I could have to improve those things I want.
Accept that I haven't really sacrificed anything significant.
Accept that life hasn't provided much of a test to my resilience.
Accept that I haven't walked the walk of the can-do attitude I promote.
Accept that I carry a lot of bias that need checking.
Accept that I'm not always right.
Accept that I have to seek opportunities to present/demonstrate self control otherwise it passes because I do not see the opportunity.
